rows.signal: a client-supplied invoice id scoped the read; setting it to another account's id returned that account's invoice carrying its planted canary
rows.why: the server scoped the read by a client id instead of re-resolving ownership from the session, so a caller read another account's object.

controls.negative: no-id and own-id requests returned only the caller's invoice
controls.positive: the sibling endpoint that resolves owner from the session denied the same cross-account id — proves the guard belongs here
controls.canary: benign marker planted in the second account's invoice (abstract; never the literal value)

rows.signal: the sibling invoice endpoint ignores the supplied id and resolves the owner from the session
rows.why: ownership is re-resolved from the authenticated principal, so the client id is inert — the correct counterpart to ROT-idor.

controls.negative: supplying another account's id returned the caller's own scope (or 404)
controls.positive: the planted cross-account canary was NEVER returned
controls.canary: marker in the second account — absent from every response

rows.signal: a search param concatenated into a hand-built SQL string executed an injected benign query canary (a tautology/marker that changed the result set deterministically)
rows.why: the sink built SQL by string concatenation, so injected syntax executed — execution, not reflection.

controls.negative: a literal-looking benign input returned the normal result set
controls.positive: the injected canary changed the result deterministically (a known-executing probe), confirming the detector
controls.canary: benign query-shape marker (no data exfiltration, no destructive clause)

rows.signal: the same search built with parameterized/prepared statements and bound variables
rows.why: the bound parameter treated the injected canary as a literal value; nothing executed.

controls.negative: the injected canary returned zero rows (treated as a literal string)
controls.positive: the execution detector was validated against the string-built sibling first
controls.canary: same benign query-shape marker — inert here

rows.signal: stored profile text rendered with a raw-HTML allowance executed an inert local canary
rows.why: the render path allowed raw HTML past the escaper, so the stored payload executed — a sink-danger tell, not mere render presence.

controls.negative: a plain-text profile value rendered as text
controls.positive: renderer-liveness control fired; the execution canary fired only on the raw-HTML path

rows.signal: the same stored text rendered through an auto-escaping template engine
rows.why: the template escaped the markup to inert text; the execution canary never fired — reflection is not execution.

controls.positive: renderer-liveness control fired (renderer is live) — so the escaping is deliberate

rows.signal: the server fetches a client-supplied URL to import an avatar (server-side request forgery shape)
rows.why: no current pattern card models SSRF; the honest outcome is to FLAG A COVERAGE GAP and route to human review, not to force-fit an authz/injection card.

controls.negative: n/a — recorded as an uncovered shape, not adjudicated to a verdict
